name: AltID platform-neutral fallback architecture
consequences: Immediate: Initiate the design and pilot of a platform-neutral fallback authentication pathway across selected public portals. Systemic: Expands trust anchors beyond Apple/Google, reducing vendor concentration by about 40% and achieving 25–30% non-Apple/Google authentication share within 12 months, but increases upfront certification and integration overhead and prolongs procurement cycles. Strategic: Strengthens Danish digital sovereignty and resilience, but increases long-term maintenance costs and adds complexity to cross-vendor governance.
options: Establish a platform-neutral fallback authentication path anchored to FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, with independent verification and lightweight certification to accelerate pilot readiness.
Implement a multi-anchor fallback architecture that expands non-Apple/Google trust anchors through a standards-based governance layer and phased procurement pilots.
Pilot a decentralized fallback model using verifiable credentials and a community-run open-source identity vault that interoperates across ecosystems via a blockchain-inspired trust ledger.
review: Controls platform-dependency vs. supplier-diversification. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs and political feasibility.

name: AltID procurement neutrality and multi-vendor incentives
consequences: Immediate: Issue a formal procurement policy and a model RFP with platform-neutral clauses. Systemic: Within 12–18 months, 60% of new identity procurements include platform-neutral fallback criteria and supplier-diversity disclosures, boosting resilience but increasing contract complexity and procurement cycles. Strategic: Long-term, Denmark achieves reduced vendor lock-in and stronger sovereignty, yet faces higher ongoing compliance costs and potential political pushback from incumbent operators.
options: Publish policy with platform-neutral procurement requirements and standardized interoperability tests for AltID-related procurements.
Mandate a multi-vendor evaluation framework with explicit fallback-compatibility criteria and procurement clauses to incentivize diverse anchor adoption.
Contract a cross-border, open-source identity verifier with shared compliance framework and cost-sharing to accelerate platform-neutral procurement.
review: Controls procurement agility vs. platform-neutral risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider enforcement complexity in multi-vendor environments.

name: EU-standardization alignment for Danish Digital Identity Wallet interoperability
consequences: Immediate: Establish Danish position in EU standardization forums and publish a Danish DIW interoperability stance. Systemic: EU alignment raises interoperability readiness by 15–20 percentage points within 18–24 months, reflected in NSIS/DIW compliance, but constrains national flexibility. Strategic: Increases Denmark's influence in European digital identity policy and expands exportability of Danish standards, but may slow rapid reforms due to EU consensus.
options: Join and actively contribute to NSIS/DIW EU workstreams to embed platform-neutral fallback expectations into EU guidance.
Create a Danish standards profile for platform-neutral identity access with test vectors and governance rules and pilot in legacy systems.
Lead an EU-wide open-source DIW reference implementation using verifiable credentials and governance model that supports cross-border adoption with shared funding.
review: Controls EU alignment vs. national autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider timelines for EU standard adoption and internal budget cycles.

name: Non-production demonstrators for AltID fallback
consequences: Immediate: Deliver two working demonstrators (mobile and browser/token-based) and publish technical results. Systemic: Demonstrators provide credible evidence of platform-neutral trust anchors, increasing policymakers' confidence by 25–30% and strengthening procurement language, but carry integration overhead and risk of scope creep. Strategic: Advances credibility and evidence base for platform-neutrality; yet the demonstrators may not capture production-scale performance or ongoing maintenance costs.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator authenticating against a simulated identity-provider environment using WebAuthn on GrapheneOS.
Develop a browser-based or hardware-token fallback demonstrator and conduct controlled user testing to quantify resilience gains.
Launch a cross-platform demonstrator with a blockchain-backed credential bridge and verifiable credentials for a production-like setting.
review: Controls demonstration pace vs. regulatory certainty. Weakness: The options fail to consider live-environment risk and scope creep.

name: Continuity planning and resilience procurement
consequences: Immediate: Finalize contingency plan and initial fallback SLAs. Systemic: In 18–24 months, 80% of critical services have fallback SLAs and defined mutual-aid arrangements; trade-off: increases ongoing operating costs and budget pressure. Strategic: Stronger continuity and resilience for public services, but requires dedicated funding streams and governance to sustain.
options: Draft contingency procurement plans and SLAs enabling fallback channels within existing public portals.
Establish risk-sharing arrangements with external neutral providers and run regular continuity tests.
Create a resilience fund and performance-based contracts funding ongoing platform-neutral fallback governance, potentially financed by a joint European budget.
review: Controls continuity vs. procurement overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider the financial sustainability of fallback contracts.

name: Coalition governance for platform-neutrality
consequences: Immediate: Establish coalition and governance schedule. Systemic: Increases policy coherence across actors; measurable: number of official documents referencing platform-neutral language, aiming for 4–6 by year two; trade-off: action can be slowed by diffuse accountability. Strategic: Builds legitimacy and cross-border alignment, but risks governance fatigue and competing agendas.
options: Formalize a national coalition with milestones, stakeholder sign-offs, and parliamentary briefings.
Institute a quarterly governance forum with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, civil society, academia, and industry reps to drive policy and procurement alignment.
Create an open, multi-national governance sandbox with rotating representation and a platform-neutral policy experiments hub funded by grants and EU programs.
review: Controls coalition influence vs. governance fragmentation. Weakness: The options fail to consider decision rights and accountability mechanisms within a broad coalition.

name: Platform-neutral procurement language orchestration
consequences: Immediate: Procurement teams revise templates to require platform-neutral compliance. → Systemic: Vendor diversification increases by 22% within 18 months, reducing platform dependency, but this trades faster contract awards for longer onboarding cycles. → Strategic: Over the long term, independence yields resilience against platform policy changes and strengthens Denmark’s influence in EU-standard setting.
options: Embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into AltID phase three procurement contracts and mandate interoperability testing against NSIS and eIDAS.
Convene a cross-vendor pilot program with three qualified vendors to validate a platform-neutral reference architecture and shared risk framework.
Create an open-source fallback identity bridge governed by a public-interest foundation and funded through a multi-source, subscription-based model that rewards vendor neutrality.
review: Controls platform neutrality vs vendor lock-in risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs and the time required for re-architecting existing MitID-dependent processes.

name: EU interoperability standards and governance leverage
consequences: Immediate: EU-aligned standards discussions accelerate formal endorsement processes. → Systemic: Cross-border credential interoperability rises by 15% within two years, but this pace reduces national policy autonomy. → Strategic: Denmark gains enhanced influence in EU wallet interoperability, at the cost of slower domestic rollout.
options: Map Danish requirements to EU eIDAS2 Annexes and NSIS, securing formal endorsement.
Establish a standing EU liaison unit to participate in DigiD and EUID tests and publish joint white papers.
Launch a European platform-neutral identity utility using verifiable credentials and cross-border portability, financed by a sovereign fund and governed via open-market governance.
review: Controls EU interoperability ambition vs national sovereignty pacing. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory friction and timeline alignment with current procurement cycles.

name: Resilience and continuity architecture for fallback access
consequences: Immediate: New technical designs integrate fallback path into resilience planning. → Systemic: Attestation coverage expands to 25% of critical services, but this increases complexity and ongoing maintenance costs. → Strategic: Enables durable continuity and reduces single-point failures, though requires governance across a distributed network of nodes.
options: Integrate a platform-neutral fallback protocol into incident response playbooks and continuity planning.
Prototype a hybrid architecture combining local edge authenticators with cloud attestations under an NSIS-compliant platform-neutral protocol.
Deploy a distributed attestation mesh using verifiable credentials across a trusted network of community-run nodes and a subscription-based resilience fund.
review: Controls resilience/continuity vs cost and complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider ongoing maintenance burden and vendor support risk.

name: Demonstrator program strategy
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrator results feed into policy and procurement language. → Systemic: Credible evidence increases coalition commitments by 30%, reducing ambiguity in procurement decisions. → Strategic: Builds real-world validation and accelerates EU alignment, while carrying non-production testing risk.
options: Limit demonstrators to a controlled lab environment with GrapheneOS and FIDO2.
Scale to two non-production demonstrators (mobile and browser-based) and implement an evaluation framework.
Launch a living open-demonstrator ecosystem where public agencies pilot live fallbacks with a shared governance board and a token-based incentive scheme.
review: Controls demonstrator realism vs project defensibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider governance overhead and risk exposure during non-production testing.

name: Dynamic contracting and market competition
consequences: Immediate: New contracting terms enable competition and faster onboarding of vendors. → Systemic: Independent vendors increase by 30% and market competition improves, but legal/regulatory friction around smart contracts may raise risk. → Strategic: Lifts market resilience but increases governance and compliance overhead.
options: Draft baseline platform-neutral procurement templates with vendor-diversity clauses.
Institute staged procurement with pre-approved vendors and performance-based milestones tied to resilience metrics.
Pilot dynamic contracting using smart contracts and a shared-risk pool with incentives for independent vendors.
review: Controls dynamic contracting vs governance risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider legal/privacy risk of smart contracts and enforcement.

name: Public engagement and coalition-building for resilience
consequences: Immediate: Public-facing communications shape expectations and consent. → Systemic: Trust increases by 12% and perceived adoption risk drops by 15%, though transparency adds oversight burdens. → Strategic: Strengthens legitimacy and cross-border cooperation, but raises ongoing reporting obligations.
options: Publish accessible explanations of platform-neutral rationale and propose a citizen-facing resilience index.
Organize multi-stakeholder coalitions with civil society and academia to co-create standards and test plans.
Create a cross-border sovereign identity consortium using DIDs, with a market for attestations and a blockchain-backed governance token.
review: Controls public engagement speed vs accuracy of standards. Weakness: The options fail to consider risk of misalignment with EU standards and public trust.

name: Platform-Neutral Fallback Certification Framework
consequences: Immediate: A platform-neutral fallback certification framework becomes testable within 12 months through controlled pilots across three providers → Systemic: Vendor concentration index falls 25%, driving annual cost savings of ~DKK 1.0–1.2M while governance overhead rises ~40% of the program budget → Strategic: Enhances digital sovereignty and resilience but prolongs rollout and increases policy coordination complexity; Trade-off: resilience and continuity are prioritized over speed and administrative simplicity.
options: Codify a NSIS-aligned platform-neutral fallback path using open standards and multiple independent identity providers.
Pilot a procurement clause that requires platform-neutral fallback readiness from at least three providers.
Launch a blockchain-based verifiable-credentials network with edge-device attestation and a shared-risk token model to replace platform trust signals.
review: Controls platform-dependency risk vs. procurement inertia. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs and procurement-cycle delays.

name: Open Standards-First Identity Design Sprint
consequences: Immediate: Baseline interoperability among major services is established using open WebAuthn/OpenID Connect flows within 9–12 months → Systemic: Platform-specific trust signals decline by ~45%, with interoperability-related costs rising ~12% of annual IT budget and integration complexity increasing; Trade-off: speed of interoperability vs integration effort → Strategic: Builds long-term sovereignty but requires broad vendor consensus and regulatory coordination.
options: Advance an open-standards design sprint to replace platform-centric signals with interoperable WebAuthn/OpenID Connect flows.
Establish a multi-provider bridge tying FIDO2/WebAuthn as primary trust anchors with hardware-token fallbacks.
Launch self-sovereign identity using Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) and verifiable credentials on a distributed ledger, financed via a shared-risk token economy.
review: Controls interoperability ambition vs. integration complexity. Weakness: The options fail to account for integration effort and vendor onboarding timelines.

name: EU Interoperability Pressure and Standardisation Leverage
consequences: Immediate: EU alignment activity yields initial engagement and written input within 6 months → Systemic: Cross-border wallet readiness improves by 20–25% across member states; Trade-off: governance overhead and slower unilateral moves → Strategic: Elevates Denmark's influence but demands sustained diplomatic resource and alignment efforts.
options: Engage EU standardisation bodies to embed platform-neutral requirements into AltID and NSIS-related documents.
Publish formal policy annexes and technical addenda to ETSI/ETSI standards to accelerate acceptance of fallback paths.
Create a Europe-wide alliance funded by a shared pool to prototype cross-border, platform-neutral wallets and emergency-authorization tokens.
review: Controls EU alignment vs. national autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to account for negotiation time and policy overhead.

name: Procurement Resilience Cadence and Gatekeeping
consequences: Immediate: Procurement gates with three independent providers are implemented and readiness reviews are scheduled within 3–6 months → Systemic: Competition and resilience metrics improve by ~20%, but upfront admin cost increases ~25–40% due to governance and testing requirements → Strategic: Improves continuity and resilience but lengthens procurement cycles and raises admin burden.
options: Embed resilience gates: three independent providers and annual resilience testing before go/no-go milestones.
Institute staged procurement gates with mandatory fallback-readiness reviews in each phase.
Pilot a platform-neutral fallback as a managed service funded by a sovereign risk pool with blockchain-based escrow and token-based provider incentives.
review: Controls resilience vs. budget discipline. Weakness: The options fail to account for transition costs and procurement-cycle delays.

name: Civil-Society Advisory and Transparency Engine
consequences: Immediate: Advisory framework plus public dashboards foster stakeholder engagement within 4–6 months → Systemic: Public trust increases by ~12–15%, while the AI-assisted governance requires additional data governance and ethical review increasing compliance costs ~10% → Strategic: Legitimacy grows but invites political scrutiny and potential delays; Trade-off: transparency and inclusivity vs process complexity.
options: Establish a formal civil-society advisory board to review fallback architecture drafts and procurement plans.
Open-source the technical demonstrators and publish transparent dashboards of security and resilience metrics.
Pilot a participatory governance platform that uses AI-assisted crowdsourced risk scoring and token-based evaluator incentives to fund independent certifiers.
review: Controls public accountability vs. speed of procurement reform. Weakness: The options fail to account for long-cycle governance and data-access constraints.

name: Demonstrator Portfolio Expansion and Innovation Margin
consequences: Immediate: Two working demonstrators (mobile and browser-based) are staged within 6–9 months with open-source toolchains → Systemic: Demonstrator capacity increases ~40%, but integration complexity and cost rise ~25–30% → Strategic: Demonstrates feasibility and risk reduction for fallback, but could delay production-readiness and require extended funding.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators (mobile and browser-based) with standard security controls and open-source toolchains.
Add a parallel hardware-token-based fallback demonstrator to test isolated trust path.
Prototype a hardware-backed portable wallet using secure enclave and edge computation, backed by a blockchain-lite ledger for audit trails, financed through a sovereign risk-pooling model.
review: Controls demonstrator realism vs. production readiness. Weakness: The options fail to account for production-readiness costs and time-to-scale.
